notes: Keep thy foot Or "guard your steps." What good advice! Solomon also says, avoid empty words, joking; speak advisedly

On a more positive note, the Preacher still felt that reverence for God and keeping covenants was better (and safer) than unfaithfulness, lest God be angry. He perceived that though leaders of a province oppress the poor and pervert judgment and justice, there is a higher judge. He implied that God is aware, and either some higher earthly judges or God himself will restore justice. (Ellis T. Rasmussen, A Latter-day Saint Commentary on the Old Testament, p.491)

Some Elders go to the nations and preach the Gospel of life and salvation, and return without thoroughly understanding the nature of the covenant. It is written in the Bible that every man should perform his own vows, even if to his own hurt [see Ecclesiastes 5:4–5]; in this way you will show to all creation and to God that you are full of integrity (Brigham Young, Discourses of Brigham Young, 396).

quotes.text: Some Elders go to the nations and preach the Gospel of life and salvation, and return without thoroughly understanding the nature of the covenant. It is written in the Bible that every man should perform his own vows, even if to his own hurt [see Ecclesiastes 5:4–5]; in this way you will show to all creation and to God that you are full of integrity

notes: that you will do all in your power to accomplish the Lord's will-not necessarily a specific result. The result is up to the Lord. But how we discipline ourselves is up to us" (Living by the Power of Faith [Salt Lake City: Deseret Book Co., 1985], 97).
Elder Gene R. Cook warned us that, "It destroys my character, in my judgment, if I promise to do something and then I don't keep my promise when I could have. The thing you really want to promise is
Say before the angel The word for angel also means messenger.

"The statement about resources introduces a section of proverbs and axioms concerning silver, goods, abundance, and riches; but all of them are lost at death" (Ellis T. Rasmussen, A Latter-day Saint Commentary on the Old Testament [Salt Lake City: Deseret, 1993], 491).

President Thomas S. Monson, in referring to this verse said, "As we view the disillusionment that engulfs countless thousands today, we are learning the hard way what an ancient prophet wrote out for us 3,000 years ago" (Thomas S. Monson, Ensign, Nov. 2000, 64).

quotes.text: President Thomas S. Monson, in referring to this verse said, "As we view the disillusionment that engulfs countless thousands today, we are learning the hard way what an ancient prophet wrote out for us 3,000 years ago"

notes: "'The sleep of a labouring man is sweet, whether he eat little or much: (I am glad I have not been rich-because this next line says) … but the abundance of the rich will not suffer him to sleep.' (Ecclesiastes 5:12.) "All my life I have enjoyed the blessed privilege of living with people who love to work. I rejoice in a helpmate who delights in keeping up our home. … "Someone has said, 'Happy is the man who has work he loves to do,' but somebody else has added the basic fundamental thought, 'Happy is the man who loves the work he has to do.'" (Adam S. Bennion in Conference Report, Apr. 1955, pp. 110-11.)

"You remember what the Lord has said: 'In the sweat of thy face shalt thou eat bread.' (Gen. 3:19.) And there is this wonderful passage in John. When the Savior was criticized for something he did on the Sabbath, he answered his accusers by saying, 'My Father worketh hitherto, and I work.' (John 5:17.) "And then that memorable passage from Ecclesiastes:

quotes.text: nce of the rich will not suffer him to sleep.' (Ecclesiastes 5:12.) "All my life I have enjoyed the blessed privilege of living with people who love to work. I rejoice in a helpmate who delights in keeping up our home. … "Someone has said, 'Happy is the man who has work he loves to do,' but somebody else has added the basic fundamental thought, 'Happy is the man who loves the work he has to do.'"

notes: Better Than Seeking Riches Is To Seek To Enjoy Walking With God (5.18-20).
Life Is Not Enjoyable To Even Some of the Rich (6.1-7).
